fr:mainmatter.html:p: This will also serve as a bit of notes from A Primer on Memory Consistency and Cache Coherence
Syntactical things I need to remember from Murphi docs. Most things are straightforward, but there are a few things to note:
Declarations. A declaration is a variable or a collection of variables that are used in the rules.
Rules. A rule is a transition from one state to another

html:ul.html:li: Each rule can optionally have an initial condition required to be fired
We can easily define rules for all the processors or collection of n things with ruleset.
For our use case, rules are used to define

html:ol.html:li: Controller actions
Receiving a request from the network
Retrying stalled requests

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Startstates. A startstate is the intial configuration for all our declarations.

html:ul.html:li: Must assign a value to every global variable

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Invariants. An invariant is something that Murphi checks to ensure is true at all possible configurations of the system.
Overview straight from the docs. Repeat forever:

html:ol.html:li: Find all rules whose conditions are true in the current state. (i.e. conditional expressions are true, given the current values of the global variables).
Choose one arbitrarily and execute the action, yielding a new state.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
The first thing we need to do is understand all the things that can go awry.
First in the valid-invalid protocol example we were given they only have two virtual channels. However, we need 3 channels to handle, request messages, forwarded messages, and response messages.
Let us briefly understand why this is the case. Detecting a deadlock is equivalent to detecting a cycle in a dependency graph, so it is sufficient to show a cyclic dependency.
If we issue a GetS this goes to the directory, giving us an edge N1 -> D.
Then the directory issues a Fwd-GetS to the ownder, giving us the chain N1 -> D -> N2
The owner then needs to send data to the requester, giving us the chain N1 -> D -> N2 -> N1.
Namely, the data must go through the same channel as all of the requests depending on it.
To avoid this, we partition into 3 channels:
Request: GetS, GetM, PutM
Forwarded: Fwd-GetS, Fwd-GetM, PutAck, Inv
Response: InvAck, Data
We directly enumerate these in our MessageType as MessageType: enum { GetS, -- request for shared copy GetM, -- request for modified (exclusive) copy PutS, -- evict shared copy PutM, -- evict modified copy (w/ data)
FwdGetS, -- forwarded GetS to owner FwdGetM, -- forwarded GetM to owner Inv, -- invalidation request
Data, -- data response InvAck, -- invalidation acknowledgment PutAck -- put acknowledgment }; Cache controller. We will start with MSI cache controller, given by the following table:
Notably, we are missing some of the messages in the figures below. We can handle these based on the AckCount ProcState: Record state: enum { P_Invalid, P_Shared, P_Modified, --stable states PT_IS_D, --sent GetS, waiting for Data PT_IS_DI, --sent GetS, got Inv, waiting for Data then go to I PT_IM_AD, --sent GetM (from I), waiting for Data and InvAcks PT_SM_AD, --sent GetM (from S), waiting for Data and InvAcks PT_IM_A, --got Data for GetM, waiting for remaining InvAcks PT_MI_A, --sent PutM, waiting for PutAck PT_SI_A, --sent PutS, waiting for PutAck PT_II_A --waiting for PutAck, already invalidated }; val: Value; ack: AckCount; -- tracks InvAck count End;
Kellen Kanarios2026324Remark The load, store, and replacement all only alter the states of the coherence protocol for the permanent states. Therefore, to simulate the system it is sufficient to only implement the rules that initiate these state transitions i.e. evict in modified state or load from invalid state.
Directory controller. Now for the directory controller:
Kellen Kanarios2026324Remark We actually end up adding a second transient state to the directory controller M^D. This is to handle the PutM, GetM race described below.
Weird Race Conditions
Kellen Kanarios2026324Problem When implementing the PutM rule to downgrade from modified to invalid. We must NOT get rid of the data until we have been acknowledged, so that we can still service forward requests. This is a transition to MI^A. However, we can still receive the PutAck causing us to transition to I before servicing the Fwd-GetM request. This is lost data.
Kellen Kanarios2026324Solution We must add a M^D state to the directory controller. In this state, if we get a PutM request from a node that is not our current owner we stall and wait for data to arrive. For M^D to transition to M, we must send the data back to the directory even though we are doing a three-hop, so that the directory knows the data has also been forwarded to the new owner. It is unclear to me the best way to do this without this state.
Kellen Kanarios2026324Problem
The above solution causes a deadlock, where two processors both store miss and one load misses. The "winning processor" of the store will be the second one. The first one will get the data from the directory transitioning from IM^{AD} \to IM^{A}, but the second one will then trigger a Fwd-GetM overwriting the first.
The load miss transitions to IS^D. However, when it receives an Inv it stalls according to the above figure. This blocks the Fwd-GetM from reaching the other winning processor because they are on the same VC. Thus, we have a deadlock where the owner never gets the data.
Kellen Kanarios2026324SolutionWe need to add another transient state not included in the figure. Namely, we add II^{A} that says we were in IS^{A}, but we got invalidated and now we are waiting for our PutAck before we transition to I.
Kellen Kanarios2026324Remark When the home directory is in M and we get a GetS we cannot clear owner as directed by figure. Otherwise in S^D, we will not be able to tell if the PutM is from the owner and stall, so we only remove the owner once we have received the data.

Aside from the modifications discussed above, the rest was straightforward and we now are ready to move on to MESI.
Cache controller. We will start with MESI cache controller, given by the following table:
Design choice 1. Since we allow immediate upgrade to M, without forwarding, if E upgrades to M, then the value in the directory sent to the next GetS / GetM would not have updated value. It seems we must either
not allow E to silently upgrade to M and then we do not need forwarding, or
allow E to silently upgrade to M and treat E as M basically and forward everything. Notably, neither of these have to write back on PutE, but we will go with 1 because that is how it is indicated in the figure.
Design Choice 2. In order to indicate that the directory received the first GetS we introduce a ExclusiveData message. Upon receiving, ExclusiveData in IS^D we transition to E.
Kellen Kanarios2026324Remark Once we have sent the PutE, or the PutM EI^A and MI^A are functionally the same. I include both for completeness but verified that transitioning directly to MI^A from E works the same.
Directory controller. We start with the analagous table.
Similar to the cache controller, we also add a E^D state for completion that can be absorbed into M^D introduced in the MSI protocol.
Weird Races.
Kellen Kanarios2026324ProblemFirst processor does GetS and is in IS^D but the Home directory sends it exclusive data and is now in E. Then second processor does GetM and Home issues Fwd-GetM to processor one, which arrives before data.
Kellen Kanarios2026324SolutionNeed to stall on Fwd-GetM and Fwd-GetS in IS^D until data arrives
Kellen Kanarios2026324ProblemAn even crazier race happens when we get invalidated before the Fwd-GetM or Fwd-GetS arrive. Due to our previous design decision, we transition to II^A, where if we receive a PutAck we transition to invalid. This then loses the needed forward data.
Kellen Kanarios2026324Example The timeline:
Proc_1 performs a load miss → IS^D
Home receives first GetS from Proc_1 → E and sends ExclusiveData to Proc_1
Proc_2 issues GetM → IM^AD
Home sends Inv to Proc_1
Home forwards Fwd-GetM to Proc_1
Proc_1 receives Inv → II^D
Proc_1 receives ExclusiveData → I Data is lost
Kellen Kanarios2026324Solution We once again have to use the ExclusiveData message. We can then see that we got ExclusiveData in II^D and transition to E instead of I. E will then correctly handle the Fwd-GetM or Fwd-GetS.
We are left with the following state diagrams for the cache and directory controllers respectively

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This is my blog, in which I write about a variety of topics including computer science, mathematics, and more, ranging from long-form deep dives to short, self-contained notes. See upcoming blogs and feel free to request! This blog also has a corresponding Atom feed.

Due to the unstructured nature of PhD life / knowledge work in general, I have been trying to come up with the most effective system to ensure I am Getting Things Done (ironically I found this system actually not that helpful).
Since Cal Newport is a professor (can relate to the knowledge work problem), I have been reading a lot of his stuff on productivity.

html:ul.html:li: Deep work.
Slow productivity.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: I often refer back to Atomic Habits as it intuitively just makes so much sense to me.

html:ul.html:li: Start small: starting a habit should take less than 2 minutes.
Never miss a habit twice.
Habit anchors: I am always going to run so now after I run I stretch, which has resulted in remarkable stretching consistency (see my Weeknotes).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: I have been using todoist and habitify. I decided on these tools primarily because of their API availability. I wanted to be able to add / complete / see outstanding tasks or habits for the day without the allure of everything else my browser has to offer. Also visualization for my Weeknotes.

html:ul.html:li: I had Claude write a little script to have my next tasks listed when clicked in my status bar, so I do not have to open the browser to check what I need to do today.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
It seems how to properly use these (todoist in particular) is the subject of much debate. Here, I will outline what I converged to / works for ME right now. I have broken down my system into a hierarchy:
Higher level: how to actually choose what to do.
Lower level: how to actually do the chosen task.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
This is effectively a task management system.
Work streams: I took this name from my investment banker friends (sounds cool / important). Basically, these are groupings I have made and allocated time for each day. They roughly consist of the following:
Research / work (4hrs first thing in the morning):
Active work towards new artifact or paper i.e. coding up ideas.
In the absence of ideas, studying existing relevant papers.
Deliberate practice (2hrs after run / lunch):
Name taken from Cal Newport.
As of writing this, a current example is spending ~2hrs a day writing / optimizing known kernels in CuTe / Pallas / NKI.
Basically, something I need to understand or master for research that is not novel.
Extraneous Study (30min - 1hr):
Things not directly related to research, but I am curious about.
I try to maintain study of related computer science fields. Primarily, studying a textbook in something like OS, computer architecture, etc.
Habits / daily upkeep (1hr normally end of day):
Mainly consists of reviewing Anki cards daily that come from extraneous study / deliberate practice.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
To decide on the next task, I use a priority-based system:
Things I will be working on today / until they are done. At most ONE thing per work stream.
The backlog of things to do next
An index to find things to move to priority 1 for the next day
Keep relatively small to avoid the crippling nature of choice - roughly on the order of things I want done this week.
A larger backlog of things - should be completed in the next month or so.
Things I am not working on right now, or have any need to be completed any time soon. This idea is mainly based on the MLFQ, which has been proven effective for balancing response / turnaround time. To maintain this system, I have reviews each day and week (tracking with habitify) to make sure I stay consistent.
Daily review:
For each work stream,
If I finished the allocated task for the day, get new task from priority 2.
Else, do nothing and continue with the current task.
Review inbox and move new tasks to corresponding work stream for review on Friday.
If immediately important, can move directly to priority 1 / 2.
Look at calendar for next day and timeblock work streams for the next day.
Since PhD work is so hard to predict, I have found timeblocking any further than one day in advance to be a futile effort.
Weekly review:
Typically done on Fridays, so that I can dynamically allocate time on weekends if needed.
For each work stream,
Look in remaining priority 2 and priority 3 tasks.
Define what I think a successful week would look like (e.g. if I get all these things done next week I will be happy).
Look at calendar for week to guide this decision.
Get rid of at least two of those tasks (initial plan always too ambitious).
Move / keep remaining tasks in priority 2.
Write Weeknotes for the week.

fr:title.text: What to do when actually working on a task

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: What to do when actually working on a task

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This is the harder part (and requires the most discipline). When working on a task, especially a more open-ended one like research, it is very common that a million other things come up that seem immediately important. All of the sudden, you have a great idea for the research project you haven't worked on in months.

It is crucial to collect and forget about these things until they are scheduled. This is the one thing I did take from Getting Things Done. The system should flow like water. Making this achievable is where the todoist API becomes crucial.
I have a script bound by dwm that pops up vim for me to write down the task and, on save, it sends it to my inbox in todoist.
I have more involved ones that allow me to actually allocate it to the specific work stream / priority if it is immediately obvious. Another thing that I have found useful to actually achieving this watery flow is the pomodoro technique. This basically lets me gaslight myself into thinking I can go to the other things as soon as the timer ends (which is intentionally always just close enough). By the end, I often no longer have this urge and can continue on the scheduled tasks.
This covers the math behind the online softmax algorithm required by Flash Attention (v1).
The first thing we must consider is numerical precision. Namely, the original softmax operation is given by
However, when operating with low precision data types (i.e. float16) the maximum value is 65536. Therefore, with \exp we can easily get numerically overflow.

To get around this, the online softmax algorithm adheres to the safe softmax. For safe softmax, we simply multiply the numerator and denominator by \exp (-m), where m = \max \{x_{1}, \ldots , x_n\}. This gives
Since x_i - m \leq 0, this ensures that each \exp (x_i - m) is bounded in a safe range.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Note that computing the \max over \mathbf {x} means that we must have all of the logits in a row of QK^T materialized in SMEM, or first loop over all of them to compute the max before computing the normalization constant.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The online softmax algorithm fuses the max reduction into the normalization pass, so a single sweep over the row suffices to compute the normalization with the max. The key difficulty is that the denominator depends on n for each i i.e.

Therefore, we must find a recursive relationship such that d_{i+1} = f(d_i, m_i, x_{i+1}) for some function f. This would then allow us to locally compute d_{i+1} with only the local information of current x_{i+1} and the two running statistics d_i and m_i. This is exactly the kind of algorithm that is conducive to memory tiling.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: What is f(d_i, m_i, x_{i+1})?
Consider d_{i+1} = d_i \cdot \exp (m_{i} - m_{i + 1}) + \exp (x_{i + 1} - m_{i + 1}).

This can be seen by directly rewriting d_{i + 1}.
\begin {align*} d_{i + 1} &= \sum _{j = 1}^{i+1} \exp (x_j - m_{i + 1}) \\ &= \left (\sum _{j = 1}^{i} \exp (x_j - m_{i+1})\right ) + \exp (x_{i+1} - m_{i+1}) \\ &= \left (\sum _{j = 1}^{i} \exp (x_j - m_{i})\right )\exp (m_{i} - m_{i + 1}) + \exp (x_{i+1} - m_{i+1}) \\ &= d_{i} \exp (m_{i} - m_{i + 1}) + \exp (x_{i+1} - m_{i+1}) \end {align*}

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The running denominator d is rescaled by e^{m - m_{\text {new}}} each time a new maximum is found, keeping it consistent with the safe-softmax shift.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Unfortunately, we cannot get around the second pass to compute the normalized output for \mathbf {p}. However, this is not the case in Flash Attention (v1).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Unlike in the online softmax, we are not interested in the outputted probability vector \mathbf {p}. Instead we are interested in the entire output matrix O given by
For more details see self-attention.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: In correspondence with the notation in the online softmax, we define the following: x_{i,j} = \mathbf {q}_i \cdot \mathbf {k}_j, where \mathbf {q}_i is the ith row of Q and \mathbf {k}_j is the jth row of K. Similarly, \mathbf {o}_i is the ith row of the output matrix O given by \mathbf {o}_i = \sum _{j = 1}^{n} \frac {\exp (x_{i,j} - m_n)}{d_n} \cdot \mathbf {v}_j

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Now following the template provided by the online softmax, we must find a recurrence for \mathbf {o}_i. Namely, we will consider the sequence \mathbf {o}_{i,k}' given by \mathbf {o}_{i,k}' = \sum _{j = 1}^{k} \frac {\exp (x_{i,j} - m_k)}{d_k} \cdot \mathbf {v}_j, such that \mathbf {o}_{i,n}' = \mathbf {o}_n

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
We have the following recurrence for \mathbf {o}_{i,k}':
\mathbf {o}_{i,k}' = \mathbf {o}_{i, k-1} \cdot \frac {d_{k - 1} \cdot \exp (m_{k-1} - m_{k})}{d_k} + \frac {\exp (x_{i,j} - m_k)}{d_k} \cdot \mathbf {v}_k

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Thus, we are left with the following algorithm

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: A key benefit of this algorithm is that it is entirely parallelizable across rows. This means that we can loop across entire tiles like in the GEMM case.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: To understand the innovation of the backward pass in flash attention, we must first find all of the gradients we must compute.
We will compute each gradient individually.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: \mathbf {P} and d\mathbf {P} are N \times N matrices. Materializing this is equivalent to materialing the attention matrix in the forward pass.

fr:mainmatter.html:div:
Key Insight 1: recomputing \mathbf {P}_{ij} and d\mathbf {P}_{ij}.
First, we can easily recompute \mathbf {P}_{ij} with \mathbf {Q} and \mathbf {K} (which are both N \times d and must be resident in memory) via \mathbf {P}_{ij} = \frac {\exp \left (\mathbf {Q}_{i} \cdot \mathbf {K}_{j}\right )}{\mathbf {L}_{i}}
We can apply the same trick to d\mathbf {P}_{ij} with d\mathbf {O} and \mathbf {V} (both N \times d). Namely, d\mathbf {P}_{ij} = \sum _{k} d\mathbf {O}_{ik} \mathbf {V}_{jk} = d\mathbf {O}_{i}^T \mathbf {V}_j
Never need an N \times N matrix.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: \mathbf {D} requires computing the global dot product \mathbf {P}_i \cdot d\mathbf {P}_i.

fr:mainmatter.html:div:
Key Insight 2:
To get around this, we precompute \mathbf {D} using the \mathbf {O} values from the forward pass and the current d\mathbf {O} values. In particular,
\begin {align*} \mathbf {D}_i &= \mathbf {P}_{i} \cdot d\mathbf {P}_{i} \\ &= \sum _{j} \frac {\exp \left (\mathbf {Q}_{i} \cdot \mathbf {K}_{j}\right )}{\mathbf {L}_{i}} d\mathbf {P}_{ij} \\ &= \sum _{j} \frac {\exp \left (\mathbf {Q}_{i} \cdot \mathbf {K}_{j}\right )}{\mathbf {L}_{i}} d\mathbf {O}_{i}^{T} \mathbf {V}_{j} \\ &= \sum _{j} d\mathbf {O}_{i}^{T} \left (\frac {\exp \left (\mathbf {Q}_{i} \cdot \mathbf {K}_{j}\right )}{\mathbf {L}_{i}} \cdot \mathbf {V}_{j}\right ) \\ &= d\mathbf {O}_{i}^{T} \sum _{j} \frac {\exp \left (\mathbf {Q}_{i} \cdot \mathbf {K}_{j}\right )}{\mathbf {L}_{i}} \cdot \mathbf {V}_{j} \\ &= d\mathbf {O}_{i}^{T} \mathbf {O}_{i} .\end {align*}
Thus, we can compute \mathbf {D}_i directly from the vectors d\mathbf {O}_i, \mathbf {O}_i.

html:ul.html:li: In the forward pass,

html:ol.html:li: Compute and store \mathbf {O} values in memory.
Compute and store \mathbf {L} values in memory

html:ul.html:li: Between the forward and backward pass,

html:ol.html:li: Compute d\mathbf {O}. (This is done automatically in JAX)
Use \mathbf {O} and d\mathbf {O} to compute \mathbf {D}. O(n) memory.

html:ul.html:li: In the backward pass,

html:ol.html:li: Compute d\mathbf {V}_j via \mathbf {Q}, \mathbf {K}, d\mathbf {O}. O(d) memory.
Compute d\mathbf {Q}_i via \mathbf {Q}_i, \mathbf {K}_j, \mathbf {L}_i, \mathbf {D}_i. O(d) memory.
Compute d\mathbf {K}_j via \mathbf {Q}_i, \mathbf {K}_j, \mathbf {L}_i, \mathbf {D}_i. O(d) memory.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This will also serve as a bit of notes from A Primer on Memory Consistency and Cache Coherence

Request: GetS, GetM, PutM
Forwarded: Fwd-GetS, Fwd-GetM, PutAck, Inv

not allow E to silently upgrade to M and then we do not need forwarding, or
allow E to silently upgrade to M and treat E as M basically and forward everything. Notably, neither of these have to write back on PutE, but we will go with 1 because that is how it is indicated in the figure.
Design Choice 2. In order to indicate that the directory received the first GetS we introduce a ExclusiveData message. Upon receiving, ExclusiveData in IS^D we transition to E.

Weird Races.
Kellen Kanarios2026324ProblemFirst processor does GetS and is in IS^D but the Home directory sends it exclusive data and is now in E. Then second processor does GetM and Home issues Fwd-GetM to processor one, which arrives before data.

Proc_1 performs a load miss → IS^D
Home receives first GetS from Proc_1 → E and sends ExclusiveData to Proc_1
Proc_2 issues GetM → IM^AD
Home sends Inv to Proc_1
Home forwards Fwd-GetM to Proc_1
Proc_1 receives Inv → II^D
Proc_1 receives ExclusiveData → I Data is lost
Kellen Kanarios2026324Solution We once again have to use the ExclusiveData message. We can then see that we got ExclusiveData in II^D and transition to E instead of I. E will then correctly handle the Fwd-GetM or Fwd-GetS.
We are left with the following state diagrams for the cache and directory controllers respectively

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Rapidly exploring random trees (RRT) is a very simple, elegant and widely used algorithm for motion planning. The algorithm proceeds as follows:

fr:mainmatter.html:div:
For k = 1 to K do
x_{rand} \gets \mathsf {RANDOM\_STATE}()
x_{near} \gets \mathsf {NEAREST\_NEIGHBOR}(x_{rand}, \mathcal {T})
u \gets \mathsf {SELECT\_INPUT}(x_{near})
x_{new} \gets \mathsf {NEW\_STATE}(x_{near}, u, \Delta t)
\mathcal {T}.add\_vertex(x_{new})
\mathcal {T}.add\_edge((x_{near}, u, x_{new})) return \mathcal {T}

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Given some dynamics model \dot {x} = f(x, u), \mathsf {NEW\_STATE} is some approximate integration, such as Euler integration i.e. x_{new} = x_{near} + f(x_{near}, u) \Delta t Preferable to use higher-order method like Runge-Kutta.

\mathsf {SELECT\_INPUT} is determined by whether the system is holonomic or non-holomonic. If the agent is holonomic, then u will just be the control corresponding to the difference in the two states.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: As I grow more interested in abandoning batched updates and the replay buffer, the importance of elibility traces grows with me. Due to this, I thought it finally time to re-prove / understand what makes these traces so eligible.
The reference followed is Sutton and Barto (Ch 12.).
Notes on the paper Fast Transformer Decoding: One Write-Head is All You Need.

For,
b batch dimension,
n sequence length,
d hidden dimension,
h number of heads. The total FLOPS for attention are roughly bnd^2. This comes from
3nd^2 FLOPS to compute Q = XW_Q, K = XW_K, V = XW_V.
Need to do this b times for each input in batch. and the total memory accesses are roughly \underbrace {bnd}_{X} + \underbrace {bhn^2}_{\text {softmax}} + \underbrace {d^2}_{\text {projection}}. This gives high arithmetic intensity O\left (\left (\frac {h}{d} + \frac {1}{bn}\right )^{-1}\right )

However, if we do incremental inference then we must multiply our total number of memory accesses by n i.e. bn^2d + nd^2. This gives arithmetic intensity O\left (\left (\frac {n}{d} + \frac {1}{b}\right )^{-1}\right ), which requires large batch and short sequence length.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: I believe we just ignore the softmax memory contribution in the inference case because it does not scale with n anymore as we are computing the logits just for the next token. Therefore, it becomes a \frac {d}{h} term, which we can safely ignore?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The key is that the \frac {n}{d} term comes from the bn^2d term, where we are loading h (b \times n \times d / h) K and V matrices n times. Thus, we can improve the \frac {n}{d} term by a factor of h by simply not using a different K and V matrix for each head. This is the entire idea behind MQA.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Similar to the KV-cache, we still can use h Q matrices because we do not need to load n of them into memory because only the last one matters for inference.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Group query attention is the same idea as MQA but instead of using one K and V for every head, they use a group of them. Where obviously the number of groups needs to be less than the number of heads.

html:figure.html:figcaption: Grouped query uses subset of K and V matrices.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Also inspired by this idea of reducing the dependence of K and V on the sequence length is sliding window attention.

The idea behind sliding window attention is to ensure the number of weight matrices needed for the keys K and values V does not scale with the sequence length. Intuitively, this means allowing each word to "attend" to only some fixed number of previous words rather than the whole sequence

html:figure.html:figcaption: "the" only sees "on" and "sat" rather than the full sentence.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Note that the gradient is with respect to \theta , where \theta is just the third argument to \mathsf {ELBO} as defined above. Notably, \theta _t will show up elsewhere later but will NOT be backpropagated through.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The above motivates the expectation step of the EM algorithm. Namely how can we compute the lower bound expectation for the best possible q.

Recall (or review) from the discussion on ELBO that q(z) = p(z | x; \theta ) maximizes the ELBO for a given x and \theta . Therefore, we actually want to learn q_{\phi }(z, x, \theta ) \approx p(z | x; \theta ). To do so, we can simply maximize the ELBO (duh). Formally, \phi _{t+1} = \argmax _{\phi } \mathbb {E}_{x \sim \mathcal {D}, z \sim q}\left [\log \frac {p(x, Z; \theta _t)}{q_{\phi }(Z, x, \theta _t)}\right ]

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Now this is all cool theoretically but how TF do we actually maximize this thing?
It is highly dependent on the parametrization of p and q.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
We define the problem as
p(z) \sim N(0, 1)
p(x | z; \theta ) \sim N(\mu _{\theta }(z), \sigma _{\theta }(z))
q_{\phi }(z | x, \theta ) \sim N(\mu _{\phi }(x), \sigma _\phi (x)) where \mu _{\{\phi , \theta \}}, \sigma _{\{\phi , \theta \}} are parametrized neural networks. Then we can rewrite the ELBO as (ignoring constants)
\begin {align*} \mathsf {ELBO}(x, q, \theta ) &= \mathbb {E}_{x \sim \mathcal {D}, z \sim q(\cdot \mid x, \theta )} \left [\log p(Z) - \log \frac {p(x \mid Z; \theta )}{q_{\phi }(Z, x, \theta )}\right ] \\ &= \mathbb {E}_{x \sim \mathcal {D}, z \sim q(\cdot \mid x, \theta )} \left [-\frac {Z^2}{2} - \frac {\sigma _{\phi }(x)^2}{\sigma _{\theta }(z)^2}\big ((Z - \mu _{\theta }(Z))^2 - (Z - \mu _{\phi }(x))^2\big )\right ] .\end {align*}
We face one more obstacle.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: We need to sample realization z \sim q_{\phi }(\cdot |x, \theta ). So isn't our realization z a function of \phi . How would we optimize this?
The reparametrization trick!!

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
For q_{\phi }(\cdot | x) \sim N(\mu _{\phi }(x), \sigma _{\phi }(x)). The reparametrization trick allows us to differentate through a random sample by sampling from q_{\phi }(\cdot | x) via z(\phi ) = \mu _{\phi }(x) + \sigma _{\phi }(x) \cdot \epsilon , where \epsilon \sim N(0, 1). Then, for a given sample \epsilon , we can easily compute 
abla _{\phi } z(\phi ) = 
abla _{\phi } \mu _{\phi }(x) + 
abla _{\phi }\sigma _{\phi }(x) \cdot \epsilon

Now for a given sample x_i \sim \mathcal {D}, we can get a sample z_i(\phi ) \sim q(\cdot \mid x_i, \theta ) that is differentiable with respect to \phi . Using this, we can get the stochastic gradient estimate \nabla _q \widetilde {\mathsf {ELBO}}(x, q, \theta ) = \nabla _{\phi } \left [\frac {z_i(\phi )^2}{2} - \frac {\sigma _{\phi }(x)^2}{\sigma _{\theta }(z)^2}\big ((z_i(\phi ) - \mu _{\theta }(z_i(\phi )))^2 - (z_i(\phi ) - \mu _{\phi }(x))^2\big )\right ]

Finally, we have reached the maximization step. This step is fairly straightforward after all the progress we have made. Once we have a tight lower bound i.e. q(z) \approx p(z \mid x; \theta ) then we simply fix q and maximize the ELBO for \theta i.e. in the above example and algorithm we instead compute \nabla _\theta \widetilde {\mathsf {ELBO}}(x, q, \theta ) = \nabla _{\theta } \left [\frac {z_i(\phi )^2}{2} - \frac {\sigma _{\phi }(x)^2}{\sigma _{\theta }(z)^2}\big ((z_i(\phi ) - \mu _{\theta }(z_i(\phi )))^2 - (z_i(\phi ) - \mu _{\phi }(x))^2\big )\right ] Alternating between these steps yields the expectation-maximization algorithm the ML oldheads mythologize.

Setup: We are given a joint distribution p(X, Z; \theta ), where X and Z are random variables with their joint parametrized by \theta . We are also given a dataset \mathcal {D} containing realizations of only X i.e. Z is a latent random variable.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Goal: We want to learn p(x; \theta ).
fr:mainmatter.html:p: However, p(x; \theta ) = \int p(x, z; \theta ) \mathrm {d}z = \int p(x | z; \theta )p(z) \mathrm {d}z, which is computationally intractable for most continuous Z.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Question: Can we update \theta for a given realization of x and z?
fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Motivation: Suppose we had some q(z) that we could sample realizations of Z from. Then we want to find some reasonable \ell _{\theta }, such that \theta ^* \approx \arg \max _{\theta } \mathbb {E}_{x, \sim p(x; \theta ^*), z \sim q(z)}[\ell _{\theta }(X, Z)]
Kellen Kanarios2025630Remark q(z) can potentially (and will) depend on the sample x_i. This is OK because we are given x_i's, which we can then just pass to q to get the corresponding z_i.
Why is this the case? Because then (for well-behaved \ell _{\theta }) \theta _{t + 1} = \theta _{t} - \alpha \nabla _{\theta _{t}}\ell _{\theta _{t}}(x_i, z_i) \to \theta ^* i.e. SGD is a reasonable thing to do, where the gradient is for a single sample of x_i and z_i which we have readily available from our dataset \mathcal {D} and our distribution q that we can sample from. This idea comes up a lot in optimization, such as the \log trick in the original policy gradient.

Similar to the \log trick, the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) comes from a simple multiply and divide trick. Namely, \begin {align*} \log p(x; \theta ) &= \log \int p(x, z; \theta ) \mathrm {d}z \\ &= \log \int p(x, z; \theta ) \frac {q(z)}{q(z)} \mathrm {d}z \\ &= \log \mathbb {E}_{z \sim q(z)} \frac {p(x, Z; \theta )}{q(z)} \\ &\geq \mathbb {E}_{z \sim q(z)} \log \frac {p(x, Z; \theta )}{q(z)} .\end {align*} The last inequality follows directly from Jensen's Inequality. However, note that this is only a lower bound and therefore not exactly what we want. For example, if we were to pick q \sim N(0, 1) and then optimize the ELBO for \theta we would not necessarily find a good \theta for the likelihood \log p(x; \theta ). Luckily, we can actually get the difference \log p(x; \theta ) - ELBO in closed form! Namely, \begin {align*} \log p(x; \theta ) - \mathrm {ELBO} &= \log p(x; \theta ) - \mathbb {E}_{z \sim q(z)} \log \frac {p(x, Z; \theta )}{q(z)} \\ &= \int \left (\log p(x; \theta ) - \log \frac {p(x, z; \theta )}{q(z)}\right )q(z) \\ &= \int \left (\log \frac {p(x; \theta )q(z)}{p(x, z; \theta )}\right )q(z) \\ &= \int \left (\log \frac {p(x; \theta )q(z)}{p(z | x; \theta )p(x; \theta )}\right )q(z) \\ &= \int \left (\log \frac {q(z)}{p(z | x; \theta )}\right )q(z) \\ &= \mathrm {KL}\big (q(z) \mid \mid p(z | x; \theta )\big ) .\end {align*} Thus, to tighten the lower bound we can optimize q_{\phi }(z) to try to match p(z | x; \theta ). As a sanity check, lets make sure these match when q(z) = p(z \mid x; \theta ). \begin {align*} \mathbb {E}_{z \sim q(z)} \log \frac {p(x, Z; \theta )}{q(z)} &= \int \log \frac {p(x, z; \theta )}{q(z)}\mathrm {d}z \\ &= \int \log \frac {p(x; \theta )p(z \mid x; \theta )}{p(z \mid x; \theta )}\mathrm {d}z \\ &= \int \log p(x; \theta )\mathrm {d}z \\ &= \log (x; \theta ) .\end {align*} You may be wondering how we use this algorithmically to find \theta ^*? This will be the main idea of expectation maximization. Feel free to check it out!

I have been using LazyVim for some time now, but I have now run into issues multiple times where understanding how LazyVim is doing something is far more difficult than if I had written my own setup. I allocated one day for this adventure and really just wanted to make sure I had support for \TeX , python, forester, and C/C++. Due to my (self-imposed) time constraint, I do not have the associated resources linked for each of the things discussed below. At some point, I hope to come back and more thoroughly cover each of the components.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: To my surprise, a lot of the features that I had come to take for granted were actually options set up internally by Lazyvim. For example, I was shocked with 8 space indents!! and I could not even copy from one terminal instance to another... Due to this, I went and found all of the options I liked from Lazyvim and added them to my new configuration in configs/options.lua.
For this, we will be using the defacto standard lazy.nvim. This is actually straightforward and kind of "just works". Just follow the installation guide in their documentation.

This is one of the main motivations for me making the switch. It seems nvim-cmp has finally been replaced with a new blink.cmp, so that is what we will be using.
Kellen Kanarios202534Rebuilding My (Neo)Vim Config From Scratch › Language Server Protocol It turns out there is a lot that goes into getting LSP setup correctly.
First we must actually install the language servers. To do this the easiest way, we use the mason.nvim and mason.nvim-lspconfig plugins. At some point, I might actually figure out how to set up lsp myself without lspconfig but that point is not now.
Through nvim-lspconfig, we can set up each of the servers we want to have LSP support. I just set up clangd, pyright, and texlab. This was a bit ridiculous. The first of many challenges was around import resolution in python. To remedy this, I needed to write a function to find the virtual environment directory and then set the pythonPath to the venv python binary. Previously, I think I was just using pylsp and installing it as a pip package to each python venv. I much prefer the new way, and I think pyright is overall a much better lsp.
Kellen Kanarios202534Rebuilding My (Neo)Vim Config From Scratch › Forester Completion Another necessary completion source for me is the one provided by forester.nvim. Similar to vimtex, the reference completion support is VERY useful. Obviously, I need completion when I am writing this blog!!! This was a little more involved. The first difficulty was that the completion source provided by the forester.nvim plugin was for nvim-cmp. It turns out this is a prevalent enough problem that the author of blink.cmp wrote an additional plugin blink.compat to allow for nvim-cmp completion sources. While this sounds all fine and good, nvim-compat expects plugins that return the completion source themselves, whereas in forester.nvim the completion source is just one submodule of a more feature-rich plugin. To get around this, I needed to look into the blink.compat code and find how they are registering the sources and just do it myself.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Going all the way back to the Gilles Castel blog post, I have always been partial to snippets that auto-expand. I had them set up prior to Lazyvim but with Lazyvim I had resigned to using friendly-snippets with native nvim snippets. Since I was already redoing everything, this time around I decided not to compromise. Once upon a time (right when it came out I think?) I tried out Luasnips, but it seems that they now have far more extensive features. They are also natively supported by blink.cmp!! It feels necessary that I plug the awesome repo that ports the original Ultisnips snippets to Luasnip. With this, I was able to easily add my own forester snippets!!!

A fun little thing that I had been hoping to do for awhile and is finally now possible - I can load latex snippets when inside math environments in forester!!!! To do this, I looked into the forester treesitter grammar and found the corresponding nodetypes for math envs. It was then straightforward to detect whether we were in a math env and to load the associated latex snippets.

There is a very cool add-on to anki called AnkiConnect that has an associated plugin anki.nvim. Basically, AnkiConnects allows you to make requests to Anki and receive/send useful information from/to your decks. Unfortunately, anki.nvim built-in commands didn't seem all that useful to me. However, it provided the necessary infrastructure for me to accomplish my desired workflow.

Namely, I made my own command that queries Anki for the deck names, which you can then pick from using telescope pickers. When you select one it will create a new flashcard in a specified flashcard directory under a directory created based on the deck name. You can then send this card to that deck using the existing AnkiSend command.

I also continued the snippet fun here. When writing Anki cards, you can write latex code between [latex] [/latex] delimiters. I wrote a quick function to detect whether we are in these delimiters and if we are then to load the latex snippets from the previous section. I also added some basic anki filetype plugins to insert things like these delimiters.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: compat.nvim, mason.nvim, black, isort.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: I often hear this question come up amongst performance-driven developers, and for the longest time I could never get a straight answer as to why the stack was so much better? After a long foray into the inner workings of how memory allocation is actually done, I think I finally have an answer. I do not want to forget this, so I am doing a quick write up here.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
This all comes down to what happens during each memory allocation. Prior to actually running the program, the operating system allocates some amount of memory for both the stack and the heap (I think around 2-4MB?). When you allocate memory on the stack i.e. int x = 1;
Stack just increments stack pointer.
Writes value to new address pointed to by stack pointer. When you allocate memory on the heap i.e. int *x = malloc(sizeof(int)), we must instead choose how best to fulfill the request, which is a whole research direction in itself.
One such implementation is the buddy algorithm However, malloc itself is not a system call and therefore is not terribly slow. A real issue occurs when we run out of pre-allocated heap memory. This requires invoking a system call like sbrk to increment the size of the allocated memory for the heap.
If no available pages, OS must evict and write to disk...
Formally, this is known as "heap growth". Generally, if you know how much memory you need it is good practice to just allocated all up front to minimize any potential system calls. With this, the performance difference is not too drastic :)

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A virtual channel (VC) is a separate logical buffer that shares the same physical link with other VCs. Multiple VCs on one link let the network distinguish classes of traffic — if one VC fills, others on the same link still flow.
Two motivations for VCs:

html:ul.html:li: Routing-deadlock avoidance: provide an escape channel routed via DOR.

Protocol-deadlock avoidance: separate message classes (request, response, forwarded) onto distinct VCs so a stuck response cannot block a request that would have unstuck it. See Implementing Directory-based MESI in Murphi for a worked Murphi example where 3 VCs are required for the directory protocol.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A scalable Cache coherence organization for systems too large for a shared bus. Each memory address has a home node; the home node maintains a directory entry listing which caches currently hold copies and in what state. Coherence transactions are point-to-point messages routed via the directory rather than broadcasts.
Compared to Snoop-based coherence, directory protocols trade a level of indirection (3 hops instead of 2 for some transactions) for vastly reduced bandwidth — making them viable at hundreds of cores.

Centralized vs. distributed. A single central directory is a bottleneck; production systems distribute it. Each processor's local DRAM has its own directory tracking the addresses physically present in that DRAM. Every physical address uniquely identifies its home node.
Reducing directory size. The naive directory is a sharer list per cache line:

html:ul.html:li: One-hot bitmap (256 nodes → 32 bytes per entry): 50% of memory consumed by metadata.
Limited-sharer encoding: most workloads have ≤4 sharers; track those exactly, fall back to broadcast for the uncommon case.

Sparse directories. Cache the directory entries themselves. An entry missing from the directory cache means nobody has a copy. On eviction from the directory cache, invalidate the corresponding lines.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Because directory transactions take multiple hops, intermediate states are visible — transient states like \mathrm {MI}^{A} (modified-to-invalid, waiting on acknowledgment). Concurrent transactions race; the protocol must handle every combination correctly.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: L has a dirty copy and wants to write back to H. R concurrently sends a read to H. R's state \mathrm {S}^{D} means "shared, waiting for data".
Never use NACKs in a directory protocol — they cause starvation.
See Implementing Directory-based MESI in Murphi for a worked implementation in Murphi, including the channel-separation arguments that prevent protocol-level deadlock.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Prefetching. Fetch data before requested to hide coherence latency.

Upgrade-miss detection. In migratory sharing (read then write), a naive read-miss followed by write-miss costs two coherence transactions. Detect the pattern and transition straight to a "migratory E" state on read.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: The same optimization hurts producer-consumer sharing. There, the right move on read-miss is to downgrade the previous holder rather than invalidate, so subsequent readers can fetch from the prior writer.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: In a Multicore system where every core has its own Cache hierarchy, the same memory address can live in multiple caches simultaneously. A cache coherence protocol is the mechanism that keeps those copies consistent — propagating writes, invalidating stale copies, and preserving two invariants:

html:ol.html:li: Single-writer invariant: at any moment, at most one core has permission to write a given line. No other core can read or write it concurrently.
Data-value invariant: all cores see the same order of updates to a given line.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Two organizational families:

html:ul.html:li: Snoop-based coherence — every cache watches a shared bus and reacts to other cores' traffic. Scales to ~8 cores.
Directory-based coherence — a structure tracks who has a copy of each line; coherence messages are point-to-point. Scales to hundreds of cores at the cost of an extra hop.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The concrete state machines are protocols like MSI protocol, MESI protocol, MOESI protocol, and DEC Firefly, each adding states to handle more cases or to optimize specific sharing patterns. A worked implementation of directory-based MESI in Murphi lives in Implementing Directory-based MESI in Murphi.
Notebook for EECS 570 taught by Prof. Naryasanamy; cross-references into Notebook: Inside the Machine (Inside the Machine) for single-core/uniprocessor topics.
The observation that as transistor dimensions shrink by a factor \alpha , you can also drop voltage by \alpha , keeping power per transistor constant:
In the Dennard era, every process shrink got more transistors at the same power budget — the foundation of decades of frequency scaling.
Post-Dennard. Below ~65nm, voltage can no longer be scaled because the supply-to-threshold ratio V_{dd}/V_{th} must stay above ~3 for transistors to switch reliably. The consequences:

html:ul.html:li: Power per transistor scales only as 1/\alpha , not 1/\alpha ^2.
Power density therefore scales as \alpha — denser chips dissipate more per unit area.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This is the same constraint that drove the Power wall in commercial CPUs: single-core frequency scaling stopped working, and the industry pivoted to multicore and energy-efficient architectures.

An alternative to fine-grained Locks: mark code regions as transactions that should appear atomic, execute them speculatively in parallel, and detect conflicts after the fact. Aims at the performance of fine-grained locking with the programming ergonomics of coarse-grained locking.
Two transactions conflict if they access the same memory location and at least one of them is a write. The implementation tracks per-transaction read and write sets.

html:ul.html:li: Lazy detection. Build the entire read/write set as the transaction runs; check for conflicts only at commit time. If conflict, roll back.
Eager detection. Check on every access against other transactions' sets; abort immediately on conflict.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Eager detection can piggyback on the cache coherence protocol: add read/write bits to each cache line, set them during a transaction, and let a coherence invalidation signal a conflict.
Two operations: commit and abort. Implementations:

html:ol.html:li: Lazy: stores go to a "write table" rather than memory; loads check the table first, falling back to memory. Commit writes the table back (slow); abort just clears the table (fast).

Eager (coherence-backed): stores write through but track read/write sets per cache line. Commit clears the sets (fast); abort clears the sets (also fast — work already on overwrite if not aborted before commit).

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: If a transaction touches more cache lines than the cache holds, the tracking overflows.
Fall back to software transactional memory (STM) on overflow — a hybrid scheme that keeps hardware fast-path for common cases.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A lock is a synchronization primitive that grants mutually exclusive access to a critical section. The implementations below trade off coherence traffic, fairness, and memory in different ways. The naive locks (Test-and-set lock and Test-and-test-and-set lock) are simple but generate O(n) coherence traffic per release; queue-based locks (Ticket lock, Array-based lock, MCS lock) scale better at the cost of extra state per waiter.

The simplest spin lock. The acquire loop spins on an atomic test_and_set: it writes LOCKED and reads the previous value in one step. If the previous value was UNLOCKED, the caller has the lock; otherwise it tries again.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Major coherence traffic. Every spinning core must acquire write permissions and invalidate everyone else on each test_and_set, so contention scales badly with the number of waiters.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A refinement of the Test-and-set lock that reduces coherence traffic during the spin. Threads first perform a plain load to check whether the lock looks free, and only attempt the atomic test_and_set when it does. Spinning is then read-only, so cores can share the cache line.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Spinning is a read-only operation now.

When the lock is released, the line must be invalidated on every waiter, and they all simultaneously read and try to write. The contention is shifted from steady state to release time but is still O(n) in the number of waiters.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A fair spin lock based on two counters. Each arriving thread takes a "ticket" by atomically incrementing next_ticket and then spins until now_serving equals its ticket. Release simply increments now_serving. Threads acquire the lock in FIFO order, which prevents starvation but does not by itself reduce contention.
fr:mainmatter.html:pre:
acquire(lock_ptr): my_ticket = fetch_and_increment(lock_ptr->next_ticket) while (lock_ptr->now_serving != my_ticket); // spin
release(lock_ptr): lock_ptr->now_serving = lock_ptr->now_serving + 1

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Still O(n) coherence traffic on release: incrementing now_serving invalidates the line on every waiter, and they all reload it to recheck their ticket.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Each waiter is assigned its own slot in an array and spins only on that slot. Release just flips the next slot to "go-ahead", so the cache line touched by release is the one read by exactly one other thread. Coherence traffic per release is constant rather than O(n), at the cost of one slot per waiter.

html:ul.html:li: Give each waiter its own "slot" to wait on.
Only need to set the next slot to "go-ahead" on release.
Constant coherence traffic.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: O(n) memory for the lock array, and padding each slot to 64b (one cache line) to avoid false sharing makes that cost real.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A scalable, list-based queue lock. The lock variable is a pointer to the tail of a linked list of per-thread nodes. Each thread enqueues its own node and spins on a flag in its own cache line. The releaser wakes only its direct successor, so coherence traffic per handoff is constant and memory cost is proportional only to threads currently waiting, not to the lock's fanout.
fr:mainmatter.html:pre:
acquire(lock): I->next = null predecessor = fetch_and_store(lock, I) if predecessor != nil: // some node holds the lock I->must_wait = true predecessor->next = I // predecessor must wake us repeat while I->must_wait // spin till lock is free
release(lock): if (I->next == null): // no known successor if compare_and_swap(lock, I, nil): // make sure... return // CAS succeeded; lock freed repeat while I->next == nil // spin to learn successor I->next->must_wait = false // wake successor

fr:mainmatter.html:p: In a Multicore system where every core has its own Cache hierarchy, the same memory address can live in multiple caches simultaneously. A cache coherence protocol is the mechanism that keeps those copies consistent — propagating writes, invalidating stale copies, and preserving two invariants:

html:ol.html:li: Single-writer invariant: at any moment, at most one core has permission to write a given line. No other core can read or write it concurrently.
Data-value invariant: all cores see the same order of updates to a given line.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Two organizational families:

html:ul.html:li: Snoop-based coherence — every cache watches a shared bus and reacts to other cores' traffic. Scales to ~8 cores.
Directory-based coherence — a structure tracks who has a copy of each line; coherence messages are point-to-point. Scales to hundreds of cores at the cost of an extra hop.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The concrete state machines are protocols like MSI protocol, MESI protocol, MOESI protocol, and DEC Firefly, each adding states to handle more cases or to optimize specific sharing patterns. A worked implementation of directory-based MESI in Murphi lives in Implementing Directory-based MESI in Murphi.
Worked instance: Implementing Directory-based MESI in Murphi implements directory-based MESI in Murphi.

The strongest practical memory model. The result of any execution is the same as if the operations of all threads were executed in some single sequential order, and the operations of each thread appear in that order in the same sequence as the program specifies.

Equivalent operational definition: imagine a single switch that, at each step, selects one thread to run its next operation. Any valid execution is the result of some sequence of such selections. There is no reordering across threads, no reordering within a thread, and no buffering that becomes visible to other threads.

Sequential consistency is what most programmers reason about by default — and what real hardware does not provide, because it forbids almost every optimization that makes a CPU fast. Relaxed consistency is what we get in exchange.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: SC does not provide guarantees between threads beyond ordering. Two threads racing on the same variable are still racing — SC just promises a consistent global timeline.

Enforcing SC purely in hardware requires loads to wait for the Store buffer to drain and stores to retire strictly in order. Both kill performance. SC-preserving compilers try to push the constraint to compile time by refusing to reorder shared accesses.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A memory model weaker than Sequential consistency that allows the hardware (and compiler) to reorder memory operations for performance, with explicit Memory fence instructions where the programmer wants to forbid reordering.

A fence is implemented with a counter — incremented on issue of each memory op, decremented on completion. The fence stalls dispatch until the counter reaches zero, which forces the Store buffer to drain before later accesses may proceed.

Real machines pick a relaxation along a spectrum: Total store order permits only store-load reordering and is what x86 ships; Relaxed memory order permits anything and pushes the ordering burden onto the programmer. Either way, the next move is to make the fences themselves cheaper, which is what Release consistency does.

A Relaxed consistency relaxation in which loads may be hoisted above prior stores, but no other reorderings are permitted. The motivation: a store can sit in the Store buffer waiting for cache, while subsequent loads — which do not touch the buffered address — proceed without stalling.

Same-core reads of a still-buffered store get the right value through Store-to-load forwarding; cross-core reads see the old value until the buffer drains. This asymmetry is the entire point of TSO's relaxation: the store buffer hides latency from the issuing thread without forcing other threads to wait. x86 implements TSO.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Under TSO, Dekker's algorithm is still broken without Memory fence — the classic example where TSO is too weak for naive software synchronization.
The IBM 370 variant is TSO without Store-to-load forwarding — it preserves global store atomicity at the cost of same-core stall-on-self-read.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A Relaxed consistency relaxation in which any pair of memory operations may be reordered. RMO is the most permissive end of the consistency spectrum; the programmer recovers ordering only through explicit Memory fence instructions.
Each stronger reordering still has to be justified per case:

html:ul.html:li: Load → store — no performance gain to reorder; leave alone.
Store → store — keep ordering with a slightly larger Store buffer.
Load → load — speculate the second load while waiting for the first; the reorder is invisible if the speculation is verified.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Because every fence is expensive under RMO, the natural next move is to weaken the fences themselves into half-fences, which is what Release consistency does.

A Relaxed consistency discipline that recovers data-race freedom (see Data race) under Relaxed memory order not by issuing fewer fences, but by issuing weaker ones. The full-fence cost of Sequential consistency is replaced by a pair of half-fences placed exactly at the boundaries of critical sections — acquire on entry, release on exit.

The intuition is that correctness around a critical section only requires that work cannot leak out of it; work is free to sink into it. Half-fences encode exactly this asymmetry, which is why they are the right primitive for lock-based and lock-free synchronization alike.

A Memory fence across which no memory operation may move in either direction. Prior accesses must become globally visible before any subsequent access may issue. x86's mfence is the canonical example — the hammer that drains the Store buffer and serializes all prior memory operations against all subsequent ones.

Full fences are the default ordering primitive under Sequential consistency — every memory operation behaves as if a full fence sat on either side. They are also the only fence one needs to reason about correctness, which is why they are the building block for the half-fences of Release consistency.
A Memory fence that constrains motion in only one direction, splitting the symmetric guarantee of a Full fence into two cheaper primitives:

html:ul.html:li: Acquire — operations after the fence may not move above it. Used at the entry to a critical section: subsequent reads and writes cannot be hoisted before the lock is held.
Release — operations before the fence may not move below it. Used at the exit of a critical section: prior writes cannot be deferred past the unlock.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The asymmetry is exactly the freedom one wants around a critical section: expanding the section is fine — work may sink into it (past acquire) or rise into it (before release) — but work may not leak out of it. This is the basis of Release consistency and the reason C++ std::atomic exposes memory_order_acquire / memory_order_release as its core primitives.
C++ std::atomic compiles to half-fences in the right places, which is why it is the standard primitive for portable lock-free code.

A FIFO of retired-but-not-yet-globally-visible stores that decouples instruction commit from cache write. When a store reaches the head of the Reorder buffer and commits, its value has not yet reached L1 — commit only hands the store off to the store buffer. The actual cache write, called drain, happens asynchronously and may be delayed by cache misses, coherence traffic, or memory-ordering rules.

Without this split, the ROB would have to stall on a cache miss for every store. With it, once a store is known to be non-speculative, the front-end moves on while the store buffer absorbs whatever latency the memory hierarchy imposes. Commit and drain are therefore two distinct moments in the lifetime of a store:

html:ul.html:li: Commit — the store is architecturally real. Younger speculation cannot squash it, but other cores do not see it yet.
Drain — the value is written to L1 and becomes visible to the coherence protocol, and therefore to other cores.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Between these two moments the value lives only in the store buffer. Younger same-core loads can still observe it through Store-to-load forwarding, but other cores cannot — which is the structural reason Total store order permits store-load reordering across cores. A Memory fence like mfence forces the buffer to drain so that subsequent operations see a consistent global view.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Retirement bandwidth (ROB commits per cycle) and store bandwidth (drain rate to L1) are separate throughput limits. A workload that retires stores faster than the cache can absorb them eventually backs up the ROB anyway, but the store buffer absorbs short bursts at no cost.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The mechanism by which a younger load on the same core observes the value of an older store that is still sitting in the Store buffer and has not yet drained to L1. The load-store unit checks the store buffer for any in-flight store to the same address; if one exists, the load's data comes directly from the buffer entry rather than from the cache, satisfying the same-core sequential semantics that the program expects.

Forwarding is the reason the commit/drain split does not break the single-core illusion. Within one thread, a store followed by a load to the same address must read the just-stored value — and would, on a machine without a store buffer, because the cache is the single source of truth. With a store buffer, the cache is briefly stale relative to architectural state; forwarding patches that hole.

The structural consequence shows up in Total store order: store-load reordering across cores is permitted precisely because forwarding makes same-core reads see the new value, while cross-core reads continue to see the old one until drain. This asymmetry is the entire point of TSO's relaxation — the store buffer can hide latency from the issuing thread without forcing other threads to wait.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Address matching in the store buffer is partial: most implementations check enough low-order bits to detect a hit but fall back to a stall (or a re-execution) on aliasing or partial-overlap cases. Reliable wide-then-narrow forwarding is one of the harder corners of memory disambiguation.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An instruction that forces an ordering constraint between memory operations on either side of it. Fences exist because Relaxed consistency hardware reorders accesses aggressively for performance, and the programmer occasionally needs a point in the instruction stream past which prior accesses must be globally visible before subsequent ones may issue. A fence is the explicit knob for "wait until the world catches up here."
The x86 family carries three:

html:ul.html:li: sfence — store fence. All prior stores must drain from the Store buffer before any later store may issue. Used to publish a buffer whose writes must be visible to consumers in order.

lfence — load fence. All prior loads must complete before any later load issues. Less commonly needed under TSO, but useful as a speculation barrier (Spectre mitigation cases) and on architectures with load-load reordering.
mfence — full fence. Drains the entire store buffer and serializes all prior memory operations against all subsequent ones. The hammer.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: What a fence actually does in hardware is force the store buffer to drain (or wait for it to drain) rather than just waiting for Reorder buffer commit. This is the distinction worth keeping straight: ROB commit makes a store architecturally real but invisible to other cores; the fence is what makes it visible. The cost is whatever latency the cache hierarchy adds — fences are expensive precisely because they undo the latency-hiding the Store buffer exists to provide.

Under Sequential consistency every memory operation behaves as if a full fence sat on either side of it, which is why SC is fast to reason about and slow to implement. Real-world consistency models pick relaxations and require fences only at the points the programmer marks as ordering-sensitive — acquire and release Half fences (see Release consistency) are the common compromise.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: A fence does not flush caches or invalidate anything — coherence traffic does that. A fence only constrains the issue order observed by the rest of the system.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Two accesses to the same memory location, from different threads, where at least one is a write and there is no happens-before relationship between them. The happens-before relation is what synchronization primitives like Locks and atomics induce — when one access happens before another, they are not racing.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Competing for the same lock is not a data race. Whichever thread acquires first happens before the next; the access inside each critical section is sequentially ordered.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Data-race freedom (DRF) is the implicit contract behind every relaxed memory model: if your program has no data races, Relaxed consistency hardware behaves as if it were Sequential consistency. Conversely, in the presence of a data race, the result is undefined by the language and the model.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Writing data-race-free programs is hard. Treating every shared access as a synchronization point destroys performance.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: SC-preserving compilers approach: forbid the compiler from reordering shared accesses, leaving the hardware free to do TSO-style reordering. The optimization most disrupted is common-subexpression elimination across shared accesses — solved by inserting a "monitor" check on the coherence bit between expressions.

Hardware approach. Treat all pages as local/safe by default and extend page protection bits to the thread level: a non-owning thread accessing the page triggers a fault, exposing the data race at runtime.

Parallelism extracted from within a single thread by executing independent instructions concurrently. The instruction stream is sequential as written, but a sufficiently aggressive back-end can issue, execute, and complete instructions in a different order than they appear, as long as data dependencies are respected and the visible architectural state still looks in-order.
The machinery a CPU spends on ILP is the bulk of its area and complexity:

html:ul.html:li: Out-of-order execution — issue instructions whenever their operands are ready, not in program order.
Register renaming — break false dependencies by mapping the same architectural register name to different physical registers.
Reservation stations + Reorder buffer — hold dispatched instructions while waiting on operands, then commit them in original order.
Speculative execution and speculation — keep the pipeline fed across control-flow boundaries by predicting which way a branch will go.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: ILP runs into hard limits: dependency chains, branch mispredictions, and the quadratic scaling of issue-queue wakeup-select all cap how wide a single-threaded window can grow. Beyond that ceiling, more silicon stops buying more performance, which is the structural reason the industry pivoted to Thread-level parallelism for throughput-bound workloads.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: ILP is what classic uniprocessor texts mean by "out-of-order superscalar execution"; see Notebook: Inside the Machine for the unwound history.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Parallelism extracted by running multiple independent threads concurrently and switching between them whenever any one of them stalls. Where Instruction-level parallelism mines a single instruction stream for hidden concurrency, TLP gives up on that ambition and instead assumes that enough independent work exists in the program (or workload) that some thread is always ready to make progress.

TLP makes the per-thread microarchitecture cheap. There is no need for a deep issue window, aggressive speculation, or CAM-based wakeup-select: if the current thread can't proceed, the scheduler picks another. The cost shifts from per-instruction machinery to context-holding — registers, program counters, and stack pointers for every resident thread must live somewhere fast.
Two dominant implementation styles:

html:ul.html:li: Coarse-grained TLP. Conventional multicore CPUs and SMT: a handful of threads share a core, switched on long-latency events or interleaved cycle-by-cycle.
Massive TLP. GPUs keep dozens of warps resident per GPU architecture SM and switch on any stall. The GPU register scoreboard is the readiness check that makes this cheap.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: TLP fails when the workload genuinely has only one thread of useful work, or when the threads contend on shared state (locks, Cache coherence traffic). It wins overwhelmingly on data-parallel kernels where the threads are independent by construction.

The two strategies a microarchitecture can use to keep arithmetic units busy in the face of memory latency and dependencies. Instruction-level parallelism mines parallelism from within a single instruction stream; Thread-level parallelism mines it across many. Every modern design is a position on this axis, and the position determines the rest of the microarchitecture.

The choice is not really a choice — it is forced by the workload. ILP works only when the single thread has independent work to find; once dependency chains and branch mispredictions saturate the issue window, no amount of silicon spent on reorder buffers, wakeup logic, or speculation buys more throughput. TLP works only when many independent threads exist; throwing more warps at a single sequential dependency chain accomplishes nothing.
The downstream consequences split everything else:

html:ul.html:li: Control overhead. ILP machines spend most of their area on Out-of-order execution machinery — Reorder buffer, Reservation stations, Register renaming, CAM-based wakeup-select. TLP machines spend almost none; the schedule is "pick any ready thread."
Caches. ILP needs large caches because a single stalled thread blocks the whole pipeline. TLP can afford small caches because the latency is hidden by switching threads.

Register file. ILP keeps one (renamed) register set hot. TLP keeps every resident thread's register set hot simultaneously — GPU register files are an order of magnitude larger than CPU register files for this reason.
Readiness tracking. ILP pays O(N²) for CAM-based wakeup-select across N in-flight instructions; TLP pays O(1) per instruction via a per-register scoreboard.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The slogan: CPUs spend a lot of silicon to find parallelism in one thread; GPUs spend almost none because they have many threads. Everything downstream — pipeline depth, cache hierarchy, scheduling policy, energy per operation — falls out of which side of this axis the chip sits on.

Single instruction, multiple data. Extend the Fetch-execute loop so that one fetch+decode applies the same operation to a vector of operands. The fetch-decode overhead amortizes over the vector width; loop bookkeeping (increment, compare, branch) is replaced by a single vector op.
Concretely, a vector ISA adds:

html:ul.html:li: A vector register file — wider than scalar registers; specify the data width per element and total bits per register.
Vector load/store instructions for moving contiguous chunks of memory to/from vector registers efficiently.
Vector predication (masking) — apply the op only where the mask is true, allowing data-dependent control flow without scalar branches.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Trade-offs:

html:ul.html:li: Need the array length to be divisible by the vector width for perfect speedup; tail handling is annoying.
Vector pipelines cannot do Forwarding cheaply — would need O(n^2) connections to every ALU.
Wider hardware vectors can be supported on narrower hardware by microcoded decomposition; backward compatibility falls out for free.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: In practice, vector code is generated by:

html:ol.html:li: Intrinsic functions (architecture-specific C builtins).
Vector data types in a language extension.
External libraries (BLAS, etc.).
Compiler auto-vectorization.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Aliasing: modifying a vector you're also reading from causes a race within the operation.
SIMD is hardware-driven data parallelism. The GPU descendant is SIMT, which softens many of SIMD's rigidities by giving each lane its own program counter and active mask.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A GPU sacrifices single-thread latency for aggregate throughput. The whole machine is built around the Thread-level parallelism design point — keep many threads resident, switch on every stall, spend almost no silicon on per-instruction control. The opposite end of the axis from a CPU:

The GPU equivalent of a core is a streaming multiprocessor (SM). Each SM executes the same instruction across many threads in lockstep — the SIMT programming model — and uses a per-register GPU register scoreboard rather than CPU-style wakeup-select to decide which warp can issue next.

Single instruction, multiple threads. A GPU streaming multiprocessor issues one instruction per cycle to a group of threads — a Warp scheduling — but each thread within the group keeps its own program counter and register state. The hardware enforces lockstep execution within the warp while preserving the illusion of independent threads to the programmer.

SIMT is the GPU's answer to a question the Vector ISA / SIMD world struggled with: how do you get the throughput of wide vector execution without forcing the programmer to manually vectorize? Two mechanisms make this work.

When threads in a warp take different branches of a conditional, the warp does not split into smaller warps. Instead, the active mask selects which lanes execute each branch; lanes on the inactive side run dummy instructions until the warp reconverges at the post-conditional join point. The programmer writes ordinary scalar code with if/else, and the hardware silently masks the inactive lanes.

This is the chief ergonomic win over SIMD: the compiler does not have to prove that all lanes will take the same path. The cost is that divergent code pays for both sides — a 32-wide warp where 16 threads take the if-branch and 16 take the else-branch executes both branches sequentially with half the lanes idle each time.

Threads are assigned to warps based on warp-ID, but the hardware and compiler are free to regroup. JKNL re-bundles threads with similar control-flow trajectories into the same warp so that future branches stay non-divergent. This is a class of optimization that pure SIMD cannot express — a SIMD ISA fixes the lane assignment at compile time.

The hardware cost of SIMT relative to SIMD is the per-thread PC and register state. The benefit is that a single binary runs efficiently across a wide range of dynamic control flow, which is why the model has dominated GPU programming since CUDA. The scoreboard mechanism described in GPU register scoreboard operates per-warp rather than per-thread precisely because SIMT treats the warp as the unit of issue.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: SIMT is sometimes described as "SIMD with a per-lane PC," which captures the implementation but understates the programming-model impact: vectorization becomes a runtime, not a compile-time, decision.
How do GPUs deal with cache misses given they don't have OoO machinery?
Fast context-switching between warps. When a warp stalls on memory, the SM switches to another runnable warp with already-resident state. Enough warps means the SM is always busy.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: In a CPU, context switching is expensive — registers must be saved to memory. On a GPU, each SM has an enormous register file that holds the registers of all resident warps simultaneously, so a context switch is just changing which warp the scheduler picks. This is the concrete payoff of the Thread-level parallelism design choice: latency hiding becomes a scheduling problem instead of a microarchitecture problem.

The readiness-tracking structure that lets a GPU issue instructions out of program order without any of the CAM machinery a CPU needs. A scoreboard is a small table — typically one busy bit per architectural register — that records whether some in-flight instruction is still computing a value that something else might want to read.

The protocol is trivial. When an instruction issues, it sets the busy bit on its destination register. When it writes back, it clears the bit. Before any instruction can read its sources, the hardware checks the corresponding bits: if any source is busy, the instruction (or its entire warp) waits. The check is a single table lookup per source, so the per-instruction cost is O(1) regardless of how many other instructions are in flight.

This is enough because GPUs lean on Thread-level parallelism rather than Instruction-level parallelism. When a warp's next instruction reads a busy register, the GPU architecture scheduler does not stall — it picks a different ready warp from the dozens of resident warps and runs that one instead. The scoreboard's job is only to answer "is this warp ready? yes/no," which is far cheaper than the broadcast/compare a CPU Issue queue wakeup-select does to find dependency satisfaction in a single thread's instruction window.

The size difference falls out of this. CPU issue queues hold tens of entries; GPU instruction windows can hold many warps' worth of in-flight work, because every additional warp costs a row in the scoreboard and a register-file allocation, not a quadratic CAM expansion. See ILP vs TLP for why the design fork sits where it does.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: The scoreboard's simplicity is what lets GPUs spend their transistor budget on ALUs and the Register file instead of control logic. It is one of the most visible places where the Thread-level parallelism design choice cashes out as silicon savings.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A warp is 32 threads on an NVIDIA GPU executing in lockstep — one instruction issued per cycle to all 32. The SM's warp scheduler picks which resident warp to run on each cycle.

The point of the scheduler: hide stalls. When a warp issues a memory operation that misses cache, it can't run for hundreds of cycles. With many resident warps, the scheduler always has a runnable one. Aggregate throughput stays high even though individual-warp latency is awful.

Occupancy is the ratio of active warps to maximum allowed warps per SM. Higher occupancy means more candidates for the scheduler to switch to, which means more latency hiding. Low occupancy means the SM stalls because every resident warp is waiting on the same memory transaction.
For latency-bound workloads (and most GPU code is), maximizing occupancy is the first-order optimization.

When threads in a warp take different branches of a conditional, the SM must serialize: execute one branch with its threads active and the others masked off, then the other. The lanes on the inactive path execute dummy instructions, wasting their compute slots.
Mitigations:

html:ul.html:li: Structure code so threads in a warp follow the same control flow when possible.
Warp specialization: have the compiler group threads with the same control-flow pattern into the same warp.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A Graphics Processing Cluster (GPC) is a top-level hardware partition inside an NVIDIA GPU. It's one level up from the SM in the chip's hierarchy.
As of Hopper, SMs located in the same GPC are connected via an SM-to-SM network, enabling DSMEM.
NVIDIA's hardware-level term for what CUDA programmers call a thread block.

A CTA is a group of threads that execute on the same Streaming Multiprocessor (SM), can synchronize via barriers (__syncthreads()), and share fast on-chip shared memory (Shared memory (GPU)). Threads within a CTA are scheduled and executed in groups of 32 called warps.

Shared memory on a GPU is a small (per-block) on-chip scratchpad with latency comparable to L1. Software-managed — the programmer explicitly stages data through it, the hardware does not cache for them. Used to:

html:ul.html:li: Avoid non-coalesced global accesses by reorganizing data layouts on the fly.
Reuse loaded data across threads within a block.
Communicate between threads within a block.
